title: Hull City A.F.C.

infobox.value: is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), East Riding of Yorkshire, England. The club competes in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship"), the second tier of [English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). They play their home games at the [MKM Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium"), after moving from [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") in 2002.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The club's traditional home colours are black and amber, often featuring in a striped design on the shirt, hence their nickname, *the Tigers*.[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3) Hull also contest the [Humber derby](/wiki/Humber_derby "Humber derby") with both [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") and [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-HULvGRI-4)[[5]](#cite_note-HULvSCU-5)

**Hull City Association Football Club** is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), East Riding of Yorkshire, England. The club competes in the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship"), the second tier of [English football](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). They play their home games at the [MKM Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium"), after moving from [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") in 2002.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The club's traditional home colours are black and amber, often featuring in a striped design on the shirt, hence their nickname, *the Tigers*.[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3) Hull also contest the [Humber derby](/wiki/Humber_derby "Humber derby") with both [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") and [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.").[[4]](#cite_note-HULvGRI-4)[[5]](#cite_note-HULvSCU-5)

The club was founded in 1904 and was then admitted into the [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") a year later. They remained in the [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") until relegation in 1930. Hull won the [Third Division North](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North "Football League Third Division North") title in 1932–33, but were relegated three years later. They won the Third Division North under the stewardship of [Raich Carter](/wiki/Raich_Carter "Raich Carter") in 1948–49, and this time remained in the second tier for seven seasons. Having been promoted again in 1958–59, they were relegated the following season and remained in the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") until they were promoted as champions under [Cliff Britton](/wiki/Cliff_Britton "Cliff Britton") in 1965–66. Twelve seasons in the second tier culminated in two relegations in four years by 1981. They were promoted from the [Fourth Division](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") at the end of the 1982–83 campaign and were beaten finalists in the inaugural [Associate Members' Cup](/wiki/EFL_Trophy "EFL Trophy") in [1984](/wiki/1984_Associate_Members%27_Cup_final "1984 Associate Members' Cup final").

Hull were relegated in 1991 and again in 1996, but secured back-to-back promotions in 2003–04 and 2004–05. The club went on to win the [2008 play-off final](/wiki/2008_Football_League_Championship_play-off_final "2008 Football League Championship play-off final") against [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") to win a place in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") for the first time. They were relegated after two seasons, but were promoted again from the EFL Championship in 2012–13. Hull played in their first [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") final in [2014](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final"), who despite scoring twice early on, lost 3–2 to [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") after [extra-time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)"). Relegated from the Premier League the following year, they returned for a third time with victory in the [2016 play-off final](/wiki/2016_Football_League_Championship_play-off_final "2016 Football League Championship play-off final"). They were relegated again from the top-flight just a year later, before dropping into the third tier in 2020. Hull secured immediate promotion as champions of [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") at the end of the 2020–21 campaign.

## History

Further information: [History of Hull City A.F.C.](/wiki/History_of_Hull_City_A.F.C. "History of Hull City A.F.C.")

For a statistical breakdown by season, see [List of Hull City A.F.C. seasons](/wiki/List_of_Hull_City_A.F.C._seasons "List of Hull City A.F.C. seasons").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Hull_City_AFC_League_Performance.svg/250px-Hull_City_AFC_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hull_City_AFC_League_Performance.svg)

Chart showing the progress of Hull City's league finishes since the [1905–06](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "1905–06 in English football") season

### Foundation and early progress (1904–1945)

Hull City Association Football Club was founded on 28 June 1904.[[6]](#cite_note-History_webpage-6)[[7]](#cite_note-1904-1915:_The_Formative_Years-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Due to the popularity of [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), previous attempts to found an [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in the city had proved difficult.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] By 1904, both [Hull F.C.](/wiki/Hull_F.C. "Hull F.C.") and [Hull K.R.](/wiki/Hull_Kingston_Rovers "Hull Kingston Rovers") were already well-established sides with passionate local backing.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The desire for a third team to represent the city in competitive sport was not particularly present at the time, but support would soon grow.[[7]](#cite_note-1904-1915:_The_Formative_Years-7) The club faced some initial disruptions after foundation, as they had been unable to apply for membership of the [Football League](/wiki/The_Football_League "The Football League") for the [1904–05](/wiki/1904%E2%80%9305_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1904–05 Hull City A.F.C. season") season and instead played only in friendlies.[[9]](#cite_note-Hull_City_Historical_Football_Kits-9) The first of these matches was a 2–2 draw with [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.") on 1 September 1904, with a crowd of 6,000 in attendance.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] These early matches were played at Hull F.C.'s home, [the Boulevard](/wiki/The_Boulevard_(stadium) "The Boulevard (stadium)").[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3) The club's first competitive football match was in the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") preliminary round, drawing 3–3 with [Stockton](/wiki/Stockton_F.C. "Stockton F.C.") on 17 September, but they were eliminated after losing the replay 4–1 on 22 September.[[10]](#cite_note-Peterson_13-10)

After disputes with landlords at the Boulevard, Hull City temporarily moved to [the Circle](/wiki/The_Circle_(Kingston_upon_Hull) "The Circle (Kingston upon Hull)"), a [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") ground in West Park.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[7]](#cite_note-1904-1915:_The_Formative_Years-7) After having played 44 friendly fixtures the previous season, Hull City were admitted into the [Football League Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") for the [1905–06](/wiki/1905%E2%80%9306_in_English_football "1905–06 in English football") season.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) Other teams competing in the league that season included [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") and [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), as well as Yorkshire rivals [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C."), [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_A.F.C. "Bradford City A.F.C.") and [Leeds City](/wiki/Leeds_City_F.C. "Leeds City F.C.").[[10]](#cite_note-Peterson_13-10) Furthermore, [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C."), from the southern bank of the [Humber Estuary](/wiki/Humber "Humber") in [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire"), were also in the Second Division.[*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Hull and Grimsby were the only two professional teams who were granted official exemption from playing league football on [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day") because of the demands of the fish trade.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Hull defeated Barnsley 4–1 at home in their first game,[[10]](#cite_note-Peterson_13-10) and ended the season with a solid 5th-place finish.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11)

In March 1906, a permanent home ground was opened for Hull City just across the road from the cricket ground, known as [Anlaby Road](/wiki/Anlaby_Road "Anlaby Road").[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*][*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] It would house the team until 1939.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Under the guidance of [player-manager](/wiki/Player-coach "Player-coach") [Ambrose Langley](/wiki/Ambrose_Langley "Ambrose Langley"), Hull continued to finish consistently in the top-half of the table.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] They came close to promotion in the [1909–10](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_in_English_football "1909–10 in English football") season, recording what would be the club's highest-ever league finish for nearly a century. Hull had ended the season level on points with [Oldham Athletic](/wiki/Oldham_Athletic_A.F.C. "Oldham Athletic A.F.C."), but finished below the Latics due to goal average, where a narrow margin of 0.29 of a goal had meant the Tigers missed out on promotion.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11)

Hull would continue to regularly finish in the top-half of the table prior to the suspension of English football during the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), but their momentum had gone after its restart in 1919. The Tigers began to struggle, finishing in the bottom half of the table in seven seasons out of the next eleven. This culminated in [relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") to the [Third Division North](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division_North "Football League Third Division North") following the [1929–30](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_in_English_football "1929–30 in English football") season.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) Despite the league campaign ending in relegation, Hull found much better luck in the FA Cup. Prior to 2014, Hull's greatest result in any cup competition was achieved in the [1929–30 FA Cup](/wiki/1929%E2%80%9330_FA_Cup "1929–30 FA Cup").[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[13]](#cite_note-1919-1939:_Inter_War_Promise_Not_Fulfilled-13) The Tigers began with victories over the eventual champions of the Third Division, [Plymouth Argyle](/wiki/Plymouth_Argyle_F.C. "Plymouth Argyle F.C.") and the eventual champions of the Second Division, [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.").[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They then overcame [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") to meet [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") in the quarter-finals. The first game at [St James' Park](/wiki/St_James%27_Park "St James' Park") finished as a 1–1 draw, but, in the home replay, Hull beat Newcastle 1–0. This meant Hull played the semi-finals, where they were paired with [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), in a game held at the [neutral venue](/wiki/Neutral_venue "Neutral venue") of [Elland Road](/wiki/Elland_Road "Elland Road") in [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds "Leeds").[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The semi-final ended 2–2, and, so, was replayed at [Villa Park](/wiki/Villa_Park "Villa Park") in [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") four days later.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Arsenal won the semi-final replay 1–0, thus ending Hull's [cup run](/wiki/Glossary_of_association_football_terms "Glossary of association football terms").[[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b2/Hull_City30s.JPG/250px-Hull_City30s.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hull_City30s.JPG)

Hull City squad in 1936

Hull would eventually be promoted back to the Second Division after they won their first-ever league title in the [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1932–33 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Managed by [Haydn Green](/wiki/Haydn_Green "Haydn Green"), they had finished above 2nd-placed [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_A.F.C. "Wrexham A.F.C.") by just 2 points, mainly due to the goals of [Bill McNaughton](/wiki/Bill_McNaughton "Bill McNaughton") who was the league's top-scorer that season with 41 goals.

### Lower-league success and stagnation (1945–1982)

After the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the club moved to another new ground, [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park").[[14]](#cite_note-Boothferry_Park-14) In the [1948–49](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1948–49 Hull City A.F.C. season") season, under the tutelage of former England international and now [player-manager](/wiki/Player-coach "Player-coach") [Raich Carter](/wiki/Raich_Carter "Raich Carter"), Hull won promotion from the Third Division North as champions.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) "[Yo-yoing](/wiki/Yo-yo_club "Yo-yo club")" between the second and third tiers of [English football](/wiki/English_football "English football"), City had promotion seasons from the Third Division to the Second Division again in [1958–59](/wiki/1958%E2%80%9359_in_English_football "1958–59 in English football") and [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1965–66 Hull City A.F.C. season"), winning the Third Division title in the latter-season.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[15]](#cite_note-1939-1961:_The_Carter_Era_and_Beyond-15)[[16]](#cite_note-1961-1980:_Rising_under_Britton_then_Decline-16) For the majority of the 1960s, Hull was managed by [Cliff Britton](/wiki/Cliff_Britton "Cliff Britton"), who has since achieved cult-status with supporters of the club for the successes he achieved, especially the Third Division title win in 1966.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The side that year featured record club appearance-maker[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Andy Davidson](/wiki/Andy_Davidson_(footballer) "Andy Davidson (footballer)") and record club goal-scorer[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Chris Chilton](/wiki/Chris_Chilton "Chris Chilton") as well as [striker](/wiki/Forward_(association_football)#Striker "Forward (association football)") [Ken Houghton](/wiki/Ken_Houghton "Ken Houghton") and a young [Ken Wagstaff](/wiki/Ken_Wagstaff "Ken Wagstaff"), among others. It is widely regarded[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] as one of the best squads the club has ever had.

On [1 August 1970](/wiki/Hull_City_A.F.C._1%E2%80%931_Manchester_United_F.C._(1970) "Hull City A.F.C. 1–1 Manchester United F.C. (1970)"), Hull became the first team in the world to be eliminated from a cup competition on [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)"), beaten by Manchester United in the semi-final of the [Watney Cup](/wiki/Watney_Cup "Watney Cup").[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[17]](#cite_note-Shooting_from_the_hip-17)

By the early 1980s, Hull City were in the Fourth Division, and financial collapse led to [receivership](/wiki/Receivership "Receivership").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Robinson years and fall to the fourth tier (1982–1996)

Ahead of the [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_in_English_football "1982–83 in English football") season, Hull were saved from their financial troubles. [Don Robinson](/wiki/Don_Robinson_(British_businessman) "Don Robinson (British businessman)"), a businessman who had made his money as a [promoter](/wiki/Promoter_(entertainment) "Promoter (entertainment)") of [professional wrestling](/wiki/Professional_wrestling "Professional wrestling"), bought a majority stake in the club.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Robinson arrived having been chairman of [Scarborough](/wiki/Scarborough_F.C. "Scarborough F.C."), and brought the non-league side's manager, [Colin Appleton](/wiki/Colin_Appleton "Colin Appleton"), in as Smith's permanent replacement.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) That season may have seen big changes amongst the backroom staff, but on the field Hull relied on their own talents to turn things around. The early 1980s had seen a new crop of young players break into the first team, with most of them going on to do great things in football. Winger [Brian Marwood](/wiki/Brian_Marwood "Brian Marwood") made an appearance for [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") in 1988,[[21]](#cite_note-21) whilst midfielder [Steve McClaren](/wiki/Steve_McClaren "Steve McClaren") would manage the national side between 2006 and 2007.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Elsewhere, the Tigers' efficient strike partnership of notorious hard man [Billy Whitehurst](/wiki/Billy_Whitehurst "Billy Whitehurst") and creative veteran [Les Mutrie](/wiki/Les_Mutrie "Les Mutrie") had become feared throughout the Football League.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Additionally, local lad [Garreth Roberts](/wiki/Garreth_Roberts "Garreth Roberts") captained the side from midfield as Hull earned promotion with a 2nd-placed finish.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

After narrowly missing out on back-to-back promotions in May 1984, Appleton left his position at Hull, having been enticed to become the new manager of [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.").[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] His replacement was player-manager [Brian Horton](/wiki/Brian_Horton "Brian Horton") who would first join the Tigers on their summer tour of Florida the following month, where they visited [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World "Walt Disney World"), and played the [Tampa Bay Rowdies](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rowdies_(1975%E2%80%931993) "Tampa Bay Rowdies (1975–1993)"), managed by [Rodney Marsh](/wiki/Rodney_Marsh "Rodney Marsh"), in the return leg of the [Arrow Air Anglo-American Cup](/wiki/Arrow_Air "Arrow Air").[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[26]](#cite_note-A&B-26) Mark Herman would direct and edit a short [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") of the tour, with Priestman composing its music. Herman released the finished version online in 2016, titled *"A Kick in the Grass"*.[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*][[27]](#cite_note-27) Promotion followed in the [1984–85](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_in_English_football "1984–85 in English football") season under Horton,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] with the young City squad now not only talented but experienced too.

Hull remained in the Second Division for the next six years before being relegated in 1991, by which time the club's manager was [Terry Dolan](/wiki/Terry_Dolan_(football_manager) "Terry Dolan (football manager)").[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] It was during this period in the Second Division that Hull fielded a [black](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") player for the first time, when [Ray Daniel](/wiki/Ray_Daniel_(English_footballer) "Ray Daniel (English footballer)") made his debut on 23 August 1986 in a home game against [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion "West Bromwich Albion").[[28]](#cite_note-28) He would make 58 league appearances for the Tigers before moving to [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City "Cardiff City") in August 1989.

The Tigers finished 14th in the Third Division in the [1991–92](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1991–92 Hull City A.F.C. season") season, meaning that they would be competing in the new Second Division the following season.[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*][[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) In their first season in the rebranded division, Hull narrowly avoided another relegation, but the board kept faith in Dolan and over the next two seasons they achieved mid-table finishes.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Financial difficulties hampered City's progress, as key players such as [Alan Fettis](/wiki/Alan_Fettis "Alan Fettis") and [Dean Windass](/wiki/Dean_Windass "Dean Windass") had to be sold to fend off [winding-up orders](/wiki/Liquidation "Liquidation").[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[29]](#cite_note-PremierLeague-29) In the [1995–96](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1995–96 Hull City A.F.C. season") season, Hull were relegated to the Third Division.[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3)[[30]](#cite_note-1980-1997:_Robinson_the_Saviour_-_Boring,_Boring_Dolan-30)

### "The Great Escape" and rise to the top-flight (1996–2008)

The Tigers did not secure immediate promotion back to the Second Division in the [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1996–97 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign. In fact, they languished close to the bottom of the table, only ten points clear of relegation, finishing 17th.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Despite this, the season was notable due to the arrival of three important players. Firstly, [Mark Greaves](/wiki/Mark_Greaves "Mark Greaves") was bought from non-league [Brigg Town](/wiki/Brigg_Town_F.C. "Brigg Town F.C."),[[32]](#cite_note-32) before [Gregor Rioch](/wiki/Gregor_Rioch "Gregor Rioch") joined from [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C.").[[33]](#cite_note-Rioch-33) Midfielder [Warren Joyce](/wiki/Warren_Joyce "Warren Joyce") signed a permanent deal soon after, having been on loan at the club for a short time at the end of the previous season.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In July 1997, former [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") player [David Lloyd](/wiki/David_Lloyd_(tennis) "David Lloyd (tennis)"), who was the captain of the [British Davis Cup team](/wiki/Great_Britain_Davis_Cup_team "Great Britain Davis Cup team") at the time, completed a takeover of the club. This ended the Needler family's ownership, which had lasted over 50 years. He also acquired Hull F.C. (then known as [Hull Sharks](/wiki/Hull_F.C.#Summer_era "Hull F.C.")) in the same month.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Lloyd-36) Upon Lloyd's arrival, Dolan left.[[37]](#cite_note-37) He was succeeded by former [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") international [Mark Hateley](/wiki/Mark_Hateley "Mark Hateley"), who acted as player-manager.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[38]](#cite_note-38) In the [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1997–98 Hull City A.F.C. season") season Hull finished in 22nd, albeit a large enough distance above last-placed [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C.") that they were never in any real danger of relegation.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

The next campaign was a lot less comfortable. The relationship between the club's supporters and Lloyd had soured following the unveiling of plans to move the Tigers back to [the Boulevard](/wiki/The_Boulevard_(stadium) "The Boulevard (stadium)") after nearly a century away.[[36]](#cite_note-Lloyd-36) Furthermore, Lloyd also wanted to merge City and F.C. together to generate more income, a proposal that did not sit well with either fanbase. In a [League Cup](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Football_League_Cup#Second_round "1998–99 Football League Cup") fixture away at [Bolton Wanderers](/wiki/Bolton_Wanderers_F.C. "Bolton Wanderers F.C.") on 15 September 1998, Hull's travelling fans threw tennis balls onto the pitch to delay the game and show their disapproval for how their club was being run. This is often cited as the first instance of a tennis ball protest in football.[[36]](#cite_note-Lloyd-36) The club was eventually sold in November 1998, although Lloyd retained ownership of Boothferry Park. The new owners were a consortium from [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), and headed by businessman Nick Buchanan.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Their first decision was to sack Hateley, and promote the now 34-year-old Joyce to replace him as player-manager.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[41]](#cite_note-Joyce-41) When Joyce took over, the club were rooted to the bottom of the Football League.[[41]](#cite_note-Joyce-41) After a hard-fought second half of the campaign, they avoided the drop by five points.[[42]](#cite_note-42) As a result, the [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1998–99 Hull City A.F.C. season") season is now referred to as "the Great Escape" by supporters.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In October 1999, Hull signed two [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_national_football_team "Jamaica national football team") internationals in [Ian Goodison](/wiki/Ian_Goodison "Ian Goodison") and [Theo Whitmore](/wiki/Theodore_Whitmore "Theodore Whitmore"). Joyce had remembered the pair's impressive performances at the [1998 World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), and when he was presented with an opportunity to sign them, he took it. In the fourth tier, they were unstoppable. Goodison helped to anchor Hull's defence, whilst Whitmore's creativity and flair from midfield dazzled opponents.[[44]](#cite_note-Jamaica-44) Despite their talent, they could not save Joyce from losing his job. In April 2000, he was sacked and swiftly replaced by former [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.") striker [Brian Little](/wiki/Brian_Little "Brian Little").[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Within a few weeks of Little's appointment, the [1999–00](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1999–2000 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign was over, and Hull had climbed the league as high as 14th.[[46]](#cite_note-46) However, soon after the season's conclusion, Lloyd exercised his control over Boothferry Park. Due to rent issues and outstanding financial figures, he called in the [bailiffs](/wiki/Bailiff "Bailiff") and locked the club out of their own stadium.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Following a short legal battle, Hull were allowed back in the ground ahead of the upcoming [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2000–01 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

That year, the Tigers greatly improved. They achieved a play-off finish, only to lose to [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") in the semi-finals.[[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) By this time, a takeover by former [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") director [Adam Pearson](/wiki/Adam_Pearson_(sports_executive) "Adam Pearson (sports executive)") had been completed and eased the club's precarious financial situation.[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3)[[35]](#cite_note-TTY-35) Pearson began funding Hull upon his arrival, allowing Little to rebuild the team ahead of the [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2001–02 Hull City A.F.C. season") season. An overhaul amongst the attack followed, with the additions of [Gary Alexander](/wiki/Gary_Alexander_(footballer) "Gary Alexander (footballer)"),[[49]](#cite_note-GA01-49) [David Beresford](/wiki/David_Beresford "David Beresford"),[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Lawrie Dudfield](/wiki/Lawrie_Dudfield "Lawrie Dudfield"),[[51]](#cite_note-51) and [Ryan Williams](/wiki/Ryan_Williams_(footballer,_born_1978) "Ryan Williams (footballer, born 1978)").[[52]](#cite_note-52) On top of this, [Faroese](/wiki/Faroe_Islands_national_football_team "Faroe Islands national football team") international [Julian Johnsson](/wiki/Julian_Johnsson "Julian Johnsson") was signed to bolster the midfield,[[53]](#cite_note-53) whilst [Ben Petty](/wiki/Ben_Petty "Ben Petty") joined from [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") to strengthen the defensive line.[[54]](#cite_note-54) These new signings initially oversaw success, with Hull occupying a play-off spot for much of the campaign. However, following a rocky start to the new year, Little was dismissed as manager on 27 February 2002.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In the time it took to find his replacement, interim [Billy Russell](/wiki/Billy_Russell_(footballer,_born_1959) "Billy Russell (footballer, born 1959)") lost five out of his seven games in charge. [Jan Mølby](/wiki/Jan_M%C3%B8lby "Jan Mølby"), coincidentally Hull's first non-British or Irish manager, was eventually appointed on 4 April 2002.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Despite the former [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") midfielder's arrival, he could not recover the club's waning form as they slumped to 11th at the season's close.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Boothferry_park_last_day.jpg/250px-Boothferry_park_last_day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boothferry_park_last_day.jpg)

Celebrations before the final game at [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park"), 14 December 2002

The [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2002–03 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign was much the same as its predecessor. Despite its mediocrity, two key signings were made during the prior summer transfer window. Future [Hull City Hall of Fame](#Hall_of_Fame) inductees [Ian Ashbee](/wiki/Ian_Ashbee "Ian Ashbee") and [Stuart Elliott](/wiki/Stuart_Elliott_(footballer,_born_1978) "Stuart Elliott (footballer, born 1978)") arrived from [Cambridge United](/wiki/Cambridge_United_F.C. "Cambridge United F.C.") and [Motherwell](/wiki/Motherwell_F.C. "Motherwell F.C.") respectively.[[58]](#cite_note-Ashbee-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) On 11 October 2002, Mølby was sacked,[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) with [Peter Taylor](/wiki/Peter_Taylor_(footballer,_born_1953) "Peter Taylor (footballer, born 1953)") replacing him the next day.[[62]](#cite_note-62) The highlight of the season, though, was the change in stadium. After 56 years at [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park"), the Tigers played their final match there on 14 December 2002, a 1–0 loss to [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington_F.C. "Darlington F.C.").[[63]](#cite_note-63) On 18 December 2002, Hull played their first game at the new [KC Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium"), a 1–0 [friendly](/wiki/Exhibition_game "Exhibition game") win over [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."). [Steve Melton](/wiki/Steve_Melton "Steve Melton") scored the first goal at the new ground.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The first competitive match came a week later, on 26 December 2002, a 2–0 win over [Hartlepool United](/wiki/Hartlepool_United_F.C. "Hartlepool United F.C.") in the league.[[65]](#cite_note-65) There was a crowd of 22,319 spectators in attendance.[[66]](#cite_note-66) By the end of the season, there was nothing to play for, with the Tigers finishing 13th.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The [2003–04](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2003–04 Hull City A.F.C. season") season saw the beginning of one of the club's best-ever eras. Before it began, that summer's transfer window saw the signing of another future [Hull City Hall of Fame](#Hall_of_Fame) inductee, as [Andy Dawson](/wiki/Andy_Dawson "Andy Dawson") joined on a free from [Humber derby](/wiki/Humber_derby "Humber derby") rivals [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C.").[[68]](#cite_note-68) Other important arrivals that window were [Danny Allsopp](/wiki/Danny_Allsopp "Danny Allsopp"), [Richard Hinds](/wiki/Richard_Hinds "Richard Hinds"), [Jason Price](/wiki/Jason_Price "Jason Price"), and [Alton Thelwell](/wiki/Alton_Thelwell "Alton Thelwell").[[69]](#cite_note-69) Later that season, young prospects [Ryan France](/wiki/Ryan_France "Ryan France") and [Boaz Myhill](/wiki/Boaz_Myhill "Boaz Myhill") both also signed for the club.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-BM03-71) After 17 games, Hull were sat 1st in the league table.[[72]](#cite_note-72) They were eventually forced to settle for 2nd-place promotion behind [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C."), largely due to dropping vital points either side of a strong Christmas period.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The following year, in the [2004–05](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2004–05 Hull City A.F.C. season") season, the Tigers completed the impressive feat of back-to-back promotions.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The most noteworthy addition to the squad had been Hull-born veteran [Nick Barmby](/wiki/Nick_Barmby "Nick Barmby"), who arrived on a free transfer from [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.").[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Paul_Duffen_at_KC_Stadium_as_Chairmain_of_HFAFC%2C_2008_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Paul_Duffen_at_KC_Stadium_as_Chairmain_of_HFAFC%2C_2008_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_Duffen_at_KC_Stadium_as_Chairmain_of_HFAFC,_2008_(cropped).jpg)

[Paul Duffen](/wiki/Paul_Duffen "Paul Duffen"), owner of Hull City between 2007 and 2010

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Bristol_City_0-1_Hull_City.png/250px-Bristol_City_0-1_Hull_City.png)](/wiki/File:Bristol_City_0-1_Hull_City.png)

Hull City supporters prior to the [2008 play-off final](/wiki/2008_Football_League_Championship_playoff_final "2008 Football League Championship playoff final") against [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Hull_City_promotion_celebration.jpg/250px-Hull_City_promotion_celebration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hull_City_promotion_celebration.jpg)

[Phil Brown](/wiki/Phil_Brown_(footballer,_born_1959) "Phil Brown (footballer, born 1959)") and players celebrate on promotion to the Premier League in 2008

The [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2005–06 Hull City A.F.C. season") season was Hull's first in the second tier since the [1990–91](/wiki/1990%E2%80%9391_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1990–91 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign.[[11]](#cite_note-Hull_City_fchd-11) At the turn of the new year, they were in 17th-place,[[77]](#cite_note-77) only falling to 18th-place by the end of the season, finishing above the drop by 10 points and securing survival.[[78]](#cite_note-78) However, after the campaign's conclusion, manager Taylor left Hull for [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.").[[79]](#cite_note-79) He was succeeded by [Phil Parkinson](/wiki/Phil_Parkinson "Phil Parkinson"), who struggled at his new job, having overseen previous success with [Colchester United](/wiki/Colchester_United_F.C. "Colchester United F.C.").[[80]](#cite_note-80) On 4 December 2006, Parkinson was sacked with the Tigers in the relegation zone.[[81]](#cite_note-81) His replacement would prove pivotal to the next few years of the club's history. [Phil Brown](/wiki/Phil_Brown_(footballer,_born_1959) "Phil Brown (footballer, born 1959)") was announced as the new manager on 4 January 2007, having worked as interim boss alongside his new assistant [Colin Murphy](/wiki/Colin_Murphy_(footballer,_born_1944) "Colin Murphy (footballer, born 1944)") since Parkinson's dismissal.[[82]](#cite_note-82) In that time, Brown and Murphy had accelerated Hull up to 21st-place and away from immediate danger.[[83]](#cite_note-83) On 17 January 2007, local legend [Dean Windass](/wiki/Dean_Windass "Dean Windass") made a return to the Tigers, joining on loan from [Bradford City](/wiki/Bradford_City_F.C. "Bradford City F.C.") for the remainder of the [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2006–07 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.[[84]](#cite_note-84) His eight goals helped secure Hull's Championship status, with a 21st-placed finish.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Ahead of the [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2007–08 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign, Windass' move was made permanent.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Other key signings made in the transfer window included forward [Caleb Folan](/wiki/Caleb_Folan "Caleb Folan")[[88]](#cite_note-88) and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") international [Richard Garcia](/wiki/Richard_Garcia "Richard Garcia"), with the latter arriving from Brown's old club, Colchester.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The club only managed to afford these transfers through new ownership. On 11 June 2007, Adam Pearson had sold the club for a reported £13m to a consortium led by [Paul Duffen](/wiki/Paul_Duffen "Paul Duffen").[[90]](#cite_note-Duffen-90) [Russell Bartlett](/wiki/Russell_Bartlett "Russell Bartlett") and [Martin Walker](/wiki/Martin_Walker_(football_director) "Martin Walker (football director)") were also involved in the takeover.[[90]](#cite_note-Duffen-90) In response to the news, Pearson said that during his tenure he "had taken the club as far as [he] could".[[91]](#cite_note-91)

On the pitch, things started slowly. After 11 games, Hull were in 18th.[[92]](#cite_note-92) However, after gaining momentum through the winter, the Tigers were in 8th-place after 33 games, and only one point off the play-offs.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Since the start of the campaign, [Fraizer Campbell](/wiki/Fraizer_Campbell "Fraizer Campbell") had been loaned in to help push the team up the league and it was clearly working.[[94]](#cite_note-94) By the close of the season, not only had Campbell had racked up 15 goals,[[95]](#cite_note-95) but Hull had finished 3rd-place and qualified for the [play-offs](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Football_League_Championship#Play-offs "2007–08 Football League Championship").[[96]](#cite_note-96)

In the semi-finals, they faced [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."). Hull earned a 2–0 win away at [Vicarage Road](/wiki/Vicarage_Road "Vicarage Road") in the first leg, in part thanks to [John Eustace](/wiki/John_Eustace "John Eustace") receiving a red card and reducing their hosts to 10 men.[[97]](#cite_note-97) In the second leg, [Darius Henderson](/wiki/Darius_Henderson "Darius Henderson") provided a scare as Watford went 1–0 up, only for the Tigers to score a late flurry of goals and win 4–1 on the night and 6–1 on aggregate.[[98]](#cite_note-98) On 24 May 2008, [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") were the only thing between Hull and the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"). At [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), Phil Brown's men reached the top-flight for the first time in the club's history, after Dean Windass' incredible first half volley secured a famous [1–0](/wiki/2008_Football_League_Championship_play-off_final "2008 Football League Championship play-off final") win.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Hull's ascent from the fourth tier to the first tier in just five seasons was the third-fastest in English football history, behind joint-first [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") (1977–81) and [Wimbledon](/wiki/Wimbledon_F.C. "Wimbledon F.C.") (1982–86).[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Premier League football and "yo-yo" years (2008–2016)

Throughout the summer of 2008, Brown worked hard to secure new signings to improve squad depth. [Craig Fagan](/wiki/Craig_Fagan "Craig Fagan") returned from [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") for his third stint with Hull,[[101]](#cite_note-101) whilst former [Barcelona](/wiki/F.C._Barcelona "F.C. Barcelona") midfielder [Geovanni](/wiki/Geovanni "Geovanni") arrived from [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.").[[102]](#cite_note-102) Furthermore, three internationals joined the club, namely [George Boateng](/wiki/George_Boateng "George Boateng"), [Péter Halmosi](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Halmosi "Péter Halmosi"), and [Bernard Mendy](/wiki/Bernard_Mendy "Bernard Mendy").[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) Loans were also agreed for a number of players. [Marlon King](/wiki/Marlon_King "Marlon King") and [Paul McShane](/wiki/Paul_McShane_(footballer) "Paul McShane (footballer)") became Tigers for the season,[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) whilst deals for [Anthony Gardner](/wiki/Anthony_Gardner "Anthony Gardner") and [Kamil Zayatte](/wiki/Kamil_Zayatte "Kamil Zayatte") were both made permanent during the campaign.[[107]](#cite_note-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108) [Daniel Cousin](/wiki/Daniel_Cousin "Daniel Cousin") was the last major incoming transfer of the window, signing from [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C.") on 11 September 2008.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

Despite their strong transfer business, Hull were seen by many as a firm candidate for relegation.[[110]](#cite_note-110) However, on 16 August 2008, the Tigers achieved a 2–1 home victory over [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") on the opening day.[[111]](#cite_note-Fulham-111) Having gone 1–0 down inside 10 minutes, new signing Geovanni scored Hull's first goal ever in the top-flight to equalise, curling a low shot beyond [Mark Schwarzer](/wiki/Mark_Schwarzer "Mark Schwarzer") from outside the box. [Caleb Folan](/wiki/Caleb_Folan "Caleb Folan") then won the match late on, after Fagan capitalised on a defensive mistake from [Paul Konchesky](/wiki/Paul_Konchesky "Paul Konchesky").[[111]](#cite_note-Fulham-111) The [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") debutants then went on an impressive run into the early autumn, losing only one of their first nine games. This period included shock away wins over both [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")[[112]](#cite_note-112) and [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.").[[113]](#cite_note-113) In addition, after a 3–0 win away to [West Bromwich Albion](/wiki/West_Bromwich_Albion_F.C. "West Bromwich Albion F.C."),[[114]](#cite_note-114) Hull temporarily found themselves joint-second in the table, level on points with [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") and only three more behind [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.").[[115]](#cite_note-115) The Tigers had initially appeared to prove their doubters wrong, only to win just twice more all season.[[116]](#cite_note-BrownComments-116) However, after a 1–0 home loss to [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") on the final day,[[117]](#cite_note-117) Hull escaped relegation thanks to other results going in their favour.[[116]](#cite_note-BrownComments-116)

On 29 October 2009, chairman [Paul Duffen](/wiki/Paul_Duffen "Paul Duffen") resigned from his position, likely due to off-the-pitch constraints and having lost the backing of the board.[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) His predecessor, [Adam Pearson](/wiki/Adam_Pearson_(sports_executive) "Adam Pearson (sports executive)"), was reinstated to replace him on 2 November 2009.[[120]](#cite_note-120) By this point, the club's performances were very reflective of their financial situation behind the scenes. At the time of Pearson's return, Hull were sat 19th and with the worst goal difference of any team in the league.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Very little had changed by 15 March 2010,[[122]](#cite_note-122) the day manager [Phil Brown](/wiki/Phil_Brown_(footballer,_born_1959) "Phil Brown (footballer, born 1959)") was put on [gardening leave](/wiki/Gardening_leave "Gardening leave") after a run of four straight defeats.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Brown's replacement was former [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.") and [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") boss [Iain Dowie](/wiki/Iain_Dowie "Iain Dowie"),[[124]](#cite_note-124) who was announced as the club's "Temporary Football Management Consultant" two days later.[[125]](#cite_note-125) The appointment was met with some disbelief by supporters who were hoping for a stronger and more ambitious replacement.[[126]](#cite_note-126) Dowie could not keep the Tigers up, with relegation confirmed on 3 May 2010, after a 2–2 draw away at [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.").[[127]](#cite_note-127) Both Brown and Dowie had their contracts swiftly terminated after the season's conclusion.[[128]](#cite_note-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129)

[Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C.") manager [Nigel Pearson](/wiki/Nigel_Pearson "Nigel Pearson") was appointed as the Tigers' next permanent manager on 29 June 2010.[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131) With yet more financial worries as a result of relegation, Pearson was forced to be frugal in the transfer market ahead of the [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2010–11 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign. A handful of free transfers were made to cover the gaps left by departing members of the first team. The highlight of these was the acquisition of West Brom midfielder [Robert Koren](/wiki/Robert_Koren "Robert Koren"), who had just captained [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_national_football_team "Slovenia national football team") at the [2010 World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup").[[132]](#cite_note-132) On 16 December 2010, it was confirmed that [Assem Allam](/wiki/Assem_Allam "Assem Allam") had become the new owner of Hull City,[[133]](#cite_note-133) having promised to pay back club debts and eliminate any possibility of financial ruin.[[134]](#cite_note-134) This allowed Hull to spend more money in the upcoming January transfer window, which included reuniting Pearson with his star striker from his time at Leicester, [Matty Fryatt](/wiki/Matty_Fryatt "Matty Fryatt"), who arrived for £1,200,000.[[135]](#cite_note-135) The newly revitalised team set a new club record on 12 March 2011 with 14 away matches unbeaten, breaking a previous record held for over 50 years.[[136]](#cite_note-136) The streak was finally broken at 17 matches when [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") beat the Tigers 3–0 on the last day of the season at [Ashton Gate](/wiki/Ashton_Gate_(stadium) "Ashton Gate (stadium)").[[137]](#cite_note-Bristol_City_3_-_0_Hull-137) On 15 November 2011, Nigel Pearson left the club to return to Leicester.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Former player and Hull local [Nick Barmby](/wiki/Nick_Barmby "Nick Barmby") was appointed as his successor, initially as a temporary [player-manager](/wiki/Player-coach "Player-coach"), but later as the full-time head coach, after retiring from professional football in January 2012.[[139]](#cite_note-Hull_City_appoint_Nick_Barmby_as_manager-139) Barmby was sacked on 8 May 2012, after publicly criticising the club's owners in an interview.[[140]](#cite_note-140) In the same month, the club's consultancy agreement with Adam Pearson was terminated.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Up_the_Tigers_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2924934.jpg/250px-Up_the_Tigers_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2924934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Up_the_Tigers_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2924934.jpg)

[East Yorkshire Motor Services](/wiki/East_Yorkshire_Motor_Services "East Yorkshire Motor Services") bus in team colours, 2012

On 8 June 2012, [Steve Bruce](/wiki/Steve_Bruce "Steve Bruce") was appointed the club's new permanent manager on a three-year deal,[[142]](#cite_note-142) an appointment which would prove pivotal for the club's history. Bruce began by signing experienced defender [Abdoulaye Faye](/wiki/Abdoulaye_Faye "Abdoulaye Faye") from [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C."),[[143]](#cite_note-143) young winger [Sone Aluko](/wiki/Sone_Aluko "Sone Aluko") from [Rangers](/wiki/Rangers_F.C. "Rangers F.C."),[[144]](#cite_note-144) and technical midfielder [Stephen Quinn](/wiki/Stephen_Quinn "Stephen Quinn") from [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.").[[145]](#cite_note-145) Alongside loanee [Ahmed Elmohamady](/wiki/Ahmed_Elmohamady "Ahmed Elmohamady"),[[146]](#cite_note-146) this quartet would be amongst the backbone of Bruce's promotion push in his first season as manager, the [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2012–13 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign. After a 2–1 victory away at [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") on 21 December 2012, the Tigers found themselves in the league's top two for the first time that season.[[147]](#cite_note-147) Loans for [Irish](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland_national_football_team "Republic of Ireland national football team") internationals [Robbie Brady](/wiki/Robbie_Brady "Robbie Brady") and [David Meyler](/wiki/David_Meyler "David Meyler") were both made permanent in the January transfer window,[[148]](#cite_note-148) whilst striker [Gedo](/wiki/Gedo_(footballer) "Gedo (footballer)") was loaned in from Egyptian giants [Al Ahly](/wiki/Al_Ahly_SC "Al Ahly SC").[[149]](#cite_note-149) He would hit the ground running in East Yorkshire, scoring five goals in his first six games.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

Bruce would eventually guide Hull back to the Premier League, after a nervy final day of the season at home to newly crowned league champions [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") on 13 May 2013. The Tigers came from behind to lead 2–1, before [Nick Proschwitz](/wiki/Nick_Proschwitz "Nick Proschwitz") had the chance to finish the game off with a penalty in the dying embers of the match. The German saw his effort saved by [David Marshall](/wiki/David_Marshall_(Scottish_footballer) "David Marshall (Scottish footballer)"), allowing the Bluebirds to spring a counter-attack. The champions then won a penalty of their own, which was duly converted by [Nicky Maynard](/wiki/Nicky_Maynard "Nicky Maynard"). Hull would now have to rely on their Yorkshire rivals [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") to beat third-placed [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C.") at [Vicarage Road](/wiki/Vicarage_Road "Vicarage Road"). A lengthy-stoppage for an injury to Watford goalkeeper [Jonathan Bond](/wiki/Jonathan_Bond "Jonathan Bond") meant the game in [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire") was 15 minutes delayed, leaving Hull in jeopardy as they were forced to wait to find out if they had done enough for automatic promotion. With the score level at 1–1 in added time, [Ross McCormack](/wiki/Ross_McCormack "Ross McCormack") attempted to chip substitute goalkeeper [Jack Bonham](/wiki/Jack_Bonham "Jack Bonham"), and thanks to Bonham mishandling the ball, it dropped into the net, securing Leeds the win and Hull a return to the top-flight in dramatic fashion.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152)

Following promotion, Bruce set about improving the squad so it was fit to compete in the Premier League. He started by making the loans of Elmohamady and [George Boyd](/wiki/George_Boyd_(footballer) "George Boyd (footballer)") permanent,[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154) before signing [Maynor Figueroa](/wiki/Maynor_Figueroa "Maynor Figueroa"),[[155]](#cite_note-155) [Allan McGregor](/wiki/Allan_McGregor "Allan McGregor"),[[156]](#cite_note-156) and [Tom Huddlestone](/wiki/Tom_Huddlestone "Tom Huddlestone")[[157]](#cite_note-TH_&_JL-157) throughout the summer transfer window, among others. Furthermore, [Jake Livermore](/wiki/Jake_Livermore "Jake Livermore"), who had made his [England](/wiki/England_national_football_team "England national football team") debut the previous year, was loaned in for the entirety of the [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2013–14 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign.[[157]](#cite_note-TH_&_JL-157) The Tigers began the season slowly, but there were clear signs of progress. A 3–1 home victory over title-chasing Liverpool on 1 December 2013,[[158]](#cite_note-158) and a narrow 3–2 defeat to Manchester United a few weeks later,[[159]](#cite_note-159) put Hull in a good position for their upcoming home fixture against Fulham. On 28 December 2013, Hull recorded their biggest-ever Premier League win, thrashing the visitors 6–0 and lifting them into 10th place.[[160]](#cite_note-160) The game is well-remembered for Huddlestone celebrating his goal (Hull's fourth), by cutting a lock of his hair for charity having promised to grow it out until his next goal.[[161]](#cite_note-161) The promise had lasted two years, as Huddlestone had not scored since 20 April 2011, when playing for his old club, Tottenham Hotspur, in their 3–3 draw with arch-rivals Arsenal.[[162]](#cite_note-162)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Arsenal_vs_Hull_City_2014-05-17.svg/250px-Arsenal_vs_Hull_City_2014-05-17.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Arsenal_vs_Hull_City_2014-05-17.svg)

The starting line-ups for the [2014 FA Cup final](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final")

The Tigers' form dropped in January, losing all four of their league matches in the month. However, they beat [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.")[[163]](#cite_note-163) and later [Southend United](/wiki/Southend_United_F.C. "Southend United F.C.")[[164]](#cite_note-164) to progress to the fifth round of the [FA Cup](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_FA_Cup "2013–14 FA Cup"). It took two legs to defeat Championship side [Brighton & Hove Albion](/wiki/Brighton_%26_Hove_Albion_F.C "Brighton & Hove Albion F.C"),[[165]](#cite_note-165) before a 3–0 home victory over [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_F.C. "Sunderland F.C.")[[166]](#cite_note-166) and an entertaining 5–3 win against Sheffield United at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), saw Hull reach their first [FA Cup final](/wiki/FA_Cup_Final "FA Cup Final") in history.[[167]](#cite_note-167) Meanwhile, in the league they had suffered greatly since the turn of the year. Yet, despite losing their final three games of the campaign, Bruce's side avoided relegation by four points, finishing in 16th, the club's highest-ever league finish.[[168]](#cite_note-168) On 17 May 2014, Hull contested the [2014 FA Cup final](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final") with Arsenal. The Gunners were the favourites heading into the game, so when [James Chester](/wiki/James_Chester "James Chester") broke the deadlock inside five minutes, Arsenal were stunned. [Curtis Davies](/wiki/Curtis_Davies "Curtis Davies") doubled this lead moments later, leaving the opponents on the verge of capitulation. However, manager [Arsène Wenger](/wiki/Ars%C3%A8ne_Wenger "Arsène Wenger") lifted his team back off their feet and their quality eventually shone through, with [Aaron Ramsey](/wiki/Aaron_Ramsey "Aaron Ramsey") completing an incredible 3–2 comeback deep into extra-time.[[169]](#cite_note-169) Hull may have been beaten finalists, but their consolation prize was a place in the following season's [Europa League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Europa_League "2014–15 UEFA Europa League") third qualifying round. This was because cup winners Arsenal were already set to compete in the [Champions League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_UEFA_Champions_League "2014–15 UEFA Champions League").[[170]](#cite_note-170) It would be the first time that the club competed in a major European competition.[[171]](#cite_note-AST_0-0_HUL-171)

Hull's squad would need another rebuild if it was to maintain stability in the Premier League and qualify for the group stages of the Europa League. With two signings made under Pearson leaving, the now-captain Koren[[172]](#cite_note-172) and semi-prolific goalscorer Fryatt,[[173]](#cite_note-173) depth was becoming a problematic issue that Bruce would need to resolve. After a successful loan spell, the first piece of business the Tigers did that summer was bringing Livermore back on a permanent deal for a reported club record fee of around £8,000,000.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Not long after, [Robert Snodgrass](/wiki/Robert_Snodgrass "Robert Snodgrass") and [Tom Ince](/wiki/Tom_Ince "Tom Ince") arrived from [Norwich City](/wiki/Norwich_City_F.C. "Norwich City F.C.") and [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") respectively.[[175]](#cite_note-175)[[176]](#cite_note-176) Then, the double signing of [Harry Maguire](/wiki/Harry_Maguire "Harry Maguire") and [Andrew Robertson](/wiki/Andrew_Robertson "Andrew Robertson") was announced on 29 July 2014.[[177]](#cite_note-177) Two days later, Hull made their debut in European football, a 0–0 draw away to Slovak side [AS Trenčín](/wiki/AS_Tren%C4%8D%C3%ADn "AS Trenčín").[[171]](#cite_note-AST_0-0_HUL-171) The home leg saw their first European victory, a 2–1 win on 7 August 2014. Goals from Elmohamady and Aluko were enough to advance to the final play-off round.[[178]](#cite_note-178) There, they were drawn against Belgian club [K.S.C. Lokeren](/wiki/K.S.C._Lokeren_Oost-Vlaanderen "K.S.C. Lokeren Oost-Vlaanderen"). However, after a 1–0 defeat in the first leg,[[179]](#cite_note-179) a 2–1 win at home was not enough to progress due to the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule"), and Hull were eliminated from the competition.[[180]](#cite_note-180)

At the end of the summer the Tigers made a flurry of new signings and loan deals. Defender [Michael Dawson](/wiki/Michael_Dawson_(footballer) "Michael Dawson (footballer)"),[[181]](#cite_note-181) [Uruguayan](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team") international [Abel Hernández](/wiki/Abel_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Abel Hernández"),[[182]](#cite_note-182) and midfielder [Mohamed Diamé](/wiki/Mohamed_Diam%C3%A9 "Mohamed Diamé")[[183]](#cite_note-Diame,_Ramirez,_Ben_Arfa-183) all arrived permanently in the last week of the window. Furthermore, Premier League talents [Gastón Ramírez](/wiki/Gast%C3%B3n_Ram%C3%ADrez "Gastón Ramírez") and [Hatem Ben Arfa](/wiki/Hatem_Ben_Arfa "Hatem Ben Arfa") both arrived on loan.[[183]](#cite_note-Diame,_Ramirez,_Ben_Arfa-183) Although Hull had not been a success in Europe, they had started well back on home soil, being ninth place in the league after their goalless away draw with Liverpool on 25 October 2014.[[184]](#cite_note-184) However, by the time Bruce signed his second three-year deal with the club on 11 March 2015, they had dropped to 15th and were just sat above a relegation dogfight.[[185]](#cite_note-185) The situation soon became uncomfortable and Hull slipped into the relegation zone, before having their fate sealed on the final day after drawing 0–0 at home to Manchester United, with other results failing to go their way.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

Bruce stayed in spite of relegation and largely managed to keep the same squad going into the [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2015–16 Hull City A.F.C. season") season. After the 2–0 victory away at [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") on 3 November 2015, Hull found themselves atop of the Championship table.[[187]](#cite_note-187) This came a week after their [League Cup](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Football_League_Cup "2015–16 Football League Cup") penalty-shootout win over eventual Premier League champions Leicester City,[[188]](#cite_note-188) which took the Tigers to their first-ever quarter-final appearance in the competition.[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190) That appearance, on 1 December 2015, saw [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") beat Hull 4–1 at the [Etihad Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium").[[191]](#cite_note-191) Coincidentally, the Citizens would go on to win the cup later that season.[[192]](#cite_note-192) Hull would also taste glory in the 2015–16 campaign, overcoming [Derby County](/wiki/Derby_County_F.C. "Derby County F.C.") 3–2 on aggregate in the semi-finals of the [Championship play-offs](/wiki/2016_Football_League_play-offs "2016 Football League play-offs").[[193]](#cite_note-193) In the final, on 28 May 2016, a long-range effort from Diamé was enough to beat [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C."), securing the Tigers an immediate return to the top-flight.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

### Supporter unrest and steady decline (2016–2020)

On 22 July 2016, Bruce resigned from his position as manager due to an alleged rift with the club's owners and [Mike Phelan](/wiki/Mike_Phelan "Mike Phelan") was appointed interim manager.[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) Steve Bruce's four-year tenure as Hull City manager was one of the most successful in the Tigers’ history, as his team achieved two promotions to the Premier League, including the club's highest-ever league finish, as well as an FA Cup final and European football. By the summer of 2016, supporters had already become frustrated and disillusioned with the Allam family's ownership of the club,[[197]](#cite_note-197) mainly due to the failed suggestion for the club to be rebranded as *[Hull Tigers](#Name_change)*.[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Fans' desires for the club to be sold only increased after Bruce's resignation, and worries about the upcoming season were made more apparent by the fact that there had been no new first team signings since promotion. It was that point in particular that garnered the attention of [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") and national news outlets after nine of the 13 remaining fit senior players at the club jokingly posed for a squad photo whilst on their pre-season tour of [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria").[[200]](#cite_note-HDM_2016_Photo-200) At the time of the photo, Diamé, who had scored Hull's promotion-winning goal just over two months earlier, had left the club to join [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."),[[201]](#cite_note-201) whilst a permanent replacement for Bruce was still yet to be found.[[200]](#cite_note-HDM_2016_Photo-200)[[202]](#cite_note-202) An approach for [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") boss [Chris Coleman](/wiki/Chris_Coleman_(footballer) "Chris Coleman (footballer)") had reportedly been made, in response to him guiding his nation to the semi-finals of [Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016") earlier that summer,[[203]](#cite_note-203) however, this was abruptly blocked by the [Welsh FA](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Wales "Football Association of Wales").[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Leicester_Line_%2828337204214%29.jpg/250px-Leicester_Line_%2828337204214%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leicester_Line_(28337204214).jpg)

Hull City 2–1 [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City "Leicester City"), 2016

By the time the [2016–17](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2016–17 Hull City A.F.C. season") season was underway, Phelan was still in charge as interim manager. Despite low attendances at home games in protest of the Allams' ownership,[[206]](#cite_note-206) on-pitch results were surprisingly good considering the club's uncomfortable situation. This was highlighted by an unlikely opening day 2–1 win at home to [Leicester City](/wiki/Leicester_City_F.C. "Leicester City F.C."), the reigning Premier League champions at the time.[[207]](#cite_note-207)[[208]](#cite_note-208) Although good results continued until September, Hull's form drastically dipped. Despite this, on 13 October 2016, Phelan became Hull's permanent head coach, but was sacked less than 3 months later, on 3 January 2017, after little improvement.[[209]](#cite_note-209)[[210]](#cite_note-BBC_Sport-210) Two days later, [Marco Silva](/wiki/Marco_Silva "Marco Silva") was appointed as the club's new manager,[[211]](#cite_note-211) but he was unable prevent relegation at the end of the season.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

Following relegation Silva resigned,[[213]](#cite_note-213) and on 9 June 2017, the club announced [Leonid Slutsky](/wiki/Leonid_Slutsky_(football_coach) "Leonid Slutsky (football coach)") as the new head coach.[[214]](#cite_note-214) He would not last long in Hull though, with the Russian leaving by mutual consent on 3 December 2017 after a poor run of results.[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216) He was replaced by former [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") boss [Nigel Adkins](/wiki/Nigel_Adkins "Nigel Adkins"),[[217]](#cite_note-217) who led the team to avoid relegation and finish 18th at the end of the season.[[218]](#cite_note-218) Despite being in the relegation zone after 19 games of the following season, the [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2018–19 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign,[[219]](#cite_note-219) an upturn in form saw the Tigers finish in 13th place. However, Adkins resigned at the end of the season after rejecting a new contract.[[220]](#cite_note-220)

On 21 June 2019, Hull appointed [Grant McCann](/wiki/Grant_McCann "Grant McCann") as head coach on a one-year [rolling contract](/wiki/Rolling_(finance) "Rolling (finance)").[[221]](#cite_note-Hull_City_Appoint_New_Head_Coach-221) The Tigers began the [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2019–20 Hull City A.F.C. season") season well, finding themselves in 8th place after a 1–0 away win over [Sheffield Wednesday](/wiki/Sheffield_Wednesday_F.C. "Sheffield Wednesday F.C.") on 1 January 2020.[[222]](#cite_note-222) However, McCann's reliance on wingers [Jarrod Bowen](/wiki/Jarrod_Bowen "Jarrod Bowen") and [Kamil Grosicki](/wiki/Kamil_Grosicki "Kamil Grosicki") would prove fatal after both were sold by the end of that month's transfer window.[[223]](#cite_note-223)[[224]](#cite_note-224) Bowen in particular was crucial to the team's success, having scored 16 goals in 29 games so far that campaign prior to his move to [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.").[[225]](#cite_note-225) In a season delayed due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"), Hull lost 16 of their last 20 games, a run that included the club's joint-worst league defeat ever, when they lost [8–0](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C._8%E2%80%930_Hull_City_A.F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C. 8–0 Hull City A.F.C.") away at [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.").[[226]](#cite_note-BBCGrant-22Jul2020-226)[[227]](#cite_note-Wigan_defeat-227) On 22 July 2020, after losing 3–0 away to [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C."), Hull were relegated to [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One").[[228]](#cite_note-228) It would be the first time the club had played in the third tier of English football in 15 years.[[226]](#cite_note-BBCGrant-22Jul2020-226)[[229]](#cite_note-229)

### Return to the Championship and new ownership (2020–present)

Despite relegation, McCann continued as head coach for the [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2020–21 Hull City A.F.C. season") season. This decision would prove successful, as Hull were promoted back to the [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") at the first time of asking. After a 2–1 victory away at [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") on 24 April 2021, promotion was confirmed.[[230]](#cite_note-230) A week later, on the final day of the campaign, a 3–1 win at home to [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") saw the Tigers crowned [League One](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_EFL_League_One "2020–21 EFL League One") champions. It was only the fourth-ever league title that the club had won, and the most recent since the victorious [1965–66](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1965–66 Hull City A.F.C. season") Third Division campaign, 55 years prior.[[231]](#cite_note-231)

On 19 January 2022, Turkish media mogul [Acun Ilıcalı](/wiki/Acun_Il%C4%B1cal%C4%B1 "Acun Ilıcalı") and his company [Acun Medya](/wiki/Acun_Medya "Acun Medya") completed a takeover of Hull City, ending the club's controversial 11-year ownership under the Allam family.[[232]](#cite_note-Club_Statement-232) On 25 January 2022, Grant McCann was sacked,[[233]](#cite_note-233) but he was quickly replaced by [Shota Arveladze](/wiki/Shota_Arveladze "Shota Arveladze") as the new head coach two days later.[[234]](#cite_note-Shota_Arveladze_appointed_new_head_coach-234) The former [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team") international helped Hull achieve Championship survival in the [2021–22](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2021–22 Hull City A.F.C. season") season in relatively comfortable fashion,[[235]](#cite_note-235) before he too was sacked, on 30 September 2022, after four consecutive league defeats.[[236]](#cite_note-Shota_Arveladze:_Hull_City_sack_head_coach_just_hours_before_Luton_game-236)

On 3 November 2022, the club announced former player, [Liam Rosenior](/wiki/Liam_Rosenior "Liam Rosenior"), as head coach, on a two-and-a-half-year deal.[[237]](#cite_note-Rosenior_appointed_new_head_coach-237) Having strengthened both the team's defensive record and the team's away record since his arrival, Rosenior guided Hull to a 15th-placed finish at the end of the [2022–23](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2022–23 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.[[238]](#cite_note-238) Despite Rosenior overseeing a very positive [2023–24](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2023–24 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign, one where he was nominated for the EFL Championship Manager of the Season award[[239]](#cite_note-239) and had brought the Tigers within three points of a play-off place, he was sacked on 7 May 2024.[[240]](#cite_note-240) Owner Ilıcalı swiftly explained that Rosenior had been dismissed on good terms and that the departure was due to a difference in "football philosophy", with the Englishman unwilling to play the aggressive attacking style that Ilıcalı was demanding.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

With Rosenior sacked, much of the previous season's playing squad left. Among these, young talents [Jacob Greaves](/wiki/Jacob_Greaves "Jacob Greaves") and [Jaden Philogene](/wiki/Jaden_Philogene "Jaden Philogene") were now considered ready for the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"),[[242]](#cite_note-242)[[243]](#cite_note-243) and were soon bought by [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.")[[244]](#cite_note-244) and [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C.")[[245]](#cite_note-245) respectively. Other notable losses were the end of loans for [Fábio Carvalho](/wiki/F%C3%A1bio_Carvalho_(footballer,_born_2002) "Fábio Carvalho (footballer, born 2002)"), [Liam Delap](/wiki/Liam_Delap "Liam Delap"), and [Tyler Morton](/wiki/Tyler_Morton "Tyler Morton"),[[246]](#cite_note-Departing_players-246) whilst cult hero [Ozan Tufan](/wiki/Ozan_Tufan "Ozan Tufan") returned to his native [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") with [Trabzonspor](/wiki/Trabzonspor "Trabzonspor").[[247]](#cite_note-247) [Greg Docherty](/wiki/Greg_Docherty "Greg Docherty") and [Matt Ingram](/wiki/Matt_Ingram "Matt Ingram"), both key members of the squad who won promotion from [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") in 2021, also left.[[248]](#cite_note-248)[[249]](#cite_note-249)

During this period of transition, [Tim Walter](/wiki/Tim_Walter "Tim Walter") was appointed as Rosenior's replacement, beginning his new role on 1 July 2024.[[250]](#cite_note-Walter_signed-250) However, he was removed from his post on 27 November 2024, after enduring a league run of four straight defeats and nine games without a win.[[251]](#cite_note-Walter_sacked-251) At the time, Hull were 22nd in the Championship, sat just inside the relegation zone.[[252]](#cite_note-252) On 6 December 2024, [Rubén Sellés](/wiki/Rub%C3%A9n_Sell%C3%A9s "Rubén Sellés") was appointed as head coach of the club on a two-and-a-half-year deal, taking up his new role on 9 December.[[253]](#cite_note-Sellés_appointed_new_head_coach-253) Sellés was able to stave off relegation after a final day 1–1 draw away at [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C."), but it did not save his job.[[254]](#cite_note-254) He was dismissed on 15 May 2025,[[255]](#cite_note-255) before being replaced by Bosnian coach [Sergej Jakirović](/wiki/Sergej_Jakirovi%C4%87 "Sergej Jakirović") on 11 June.[[256]](#cite_note-Jakirović_appointed_new_Head_Coach-256)
