[1946–47](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_in_English_football "1946–47 in English football") home colours

*Sources*:[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3)[[9]](#cite_note-Hull_City_Historical_Football_Kits-9)[[257]](#cite_note-Kitted_Out-257)[[258]](#cite_note-258)[[259]](#cite_note-259)[[260]](#cite_note-260)

Hull City's traditional home colours are black and amber. There is a prevalent myth that the club's earliest kits featured plain white shirts with black shorts and socks. In reality, the Tigers wore black and amber striped shirts, with black shorts and socks right from the very beginning. This is highlighted by [Hull Daily Mail](/wiki/Hull_Daily_Mail "Hull Daily Mail") coining the club's nickname in March 1905, which came as a response to the kit colours.

Aside from design changes, only a handful of occasions have seen Hull's strip altered in the years since. White shorts were introduced in the early 1920s, before a more dramatic, wholesale change was made for the [1935–36](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_in_English_football "1935–36 in English football") season. Blue shirts, white shorts, and blue and white hooped socks suddenly became the club's new home kit, likely to match the civic colours of [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"). However, this strip was short-lived, as it was abandoned the following season for a return to black and amber.

Ahead of the restart of football after the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the limited supplies of dye, thanks to rationing restrictions, left the club with little choice but to return to blue shirts, albeit they were much paler than they had been a decade before. Worn with white shorts and black socks, the kit was only used in the [1946–47](/wiki/1946%E2%80%9347_in_English_football "1946–47 in English football") season.

Although once again returning to black and amber, Hull's home shirt was slightly different in the post-war era. The Tigers' iconic stripes had been dropped in favour of plain amber shirts with minimal black trim. This was until the early 1960s, when stripes were reintroduced on a semi-regular basis. The club even debuted an all-amber strip for the [1964–65](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_in_English_football "1964–65 in English football") campaign, but this was unpopular amongst supporters, and consequently retired at the end of the season. In the mid-1970s, both stripes and white shorts made welcome returns.

The next notable development in Hull's home kits was the addition of red trim in the 1980s, whose duration went no further than this. Eccentric print designs became commonplace in the 1990s, and Hull's [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1992–93 Hull City A.F.C. season") home shirt was one of the more extreme examples. Its all-over [tiger-print](/wiki/Tiger#Coat "Tiger") design divided opinions and has since featured in lists for some of the best and worst kits of all time. In the 21st century, the club have worn all sorts of designs, although usually they are either striped black and amber shirts or simply plain amber shirts along with black shorts and socks.

In 2024, a commemorative all-black strip, with gold trim, was worn for the club's 120th anniversary, in a fixture against [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C."). Precisely 1,904 shirts were available for fan purchase, as a nod to the club's founding year.

### Club crest

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/26/Hull_old_badge.png/120px-Hull_old_badge.png)](/wiki/File:Hull_old_badge.png)

1998–2001 club crest

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/20/Hull_City_Crest_2014.svg/120px-Hull_City_Crest_2014.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hull_City_Crest_2014.svg)

2014–2019 club crest

*Sources*:[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3)[[9]](#cite_note-Hull_City_Historical_Football_Kits-9)[[257]](#cite_note-Kitted_Out-257)[[261]](#cite_note-261)[[262]](#cite_note-262)

In their early history, Hull City had no crest. One was eventually introduced in the form of the three civic crowns of [Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull"), which was first embroidered onto the club's shirts for the [1935–36](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_in_English_football "1935–36 in English football") season, matching their new, city-honouring blue and white kit colours. Their next crest was the inception of the club's first tiger head, featuring inside an amber shield for the [1947–48](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_in_English_football "1947–48 in English football") campaign, having returned to black and amber kits the same year.

Variations of the tiger's head were used for the next 28 years, before the club's initials, 'HCAFC', were adopted as the official crest for the upcoming [1975–76](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_in_English_football "1975–76 in English football") season. That same year, the now-iconic tiger's head was granted as a [heraldic badge](/wiki/Heraldic_badge "Heraldic badge") by the [College of Arms](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms") to the [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League"). After four years of using the club's initials, the crest reverted to the tiger's head ahead of the [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_in_English_football "1979–80 in English football") season.

The tiger's head has been used ever since, appearing in various forms and often joined by the club's name and founding year, among other features. Before the [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2014–15 Hull City A.F.C. season") campaign, the crest was changed to just the tiger's head and '1904' inscribed beneath it, all inside an amber shield. This crest was strongly disliked by supporters for dropping the club's name, and a supporter-led redesign began after the close of the [2017–18](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2017–18 Hull City A.F.C. season") season. The process was finally concluded in February 2019, when a new crest was unveiled. It was used from the start of the [2019–20](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2019–20 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.

### Grounds

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/KC_Stadium.JPG/250px-KC_Stadium.JPG)](/wiki/File:KC_Stadium.JPG)

[MKM Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium")

Hull City initially played their home games at [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") ground, [the Boulevard](/wiki/The_Boulevard_(stadium) "The Boulevard (stadium)"), before briefly relocating to [the Circle](/wiki/The_Circle_(Kingston_upon_Hull) "The Circle (Kingston upon Hull)"), a local [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") ground.[[3]](#cite_note-History_Of_The_Tigers-3)[[7]](#cite_note-1904-1915:_The_Formative_Years-7)[[270]](#cite_note-The_Boulevard-270) Eventually, [Anlaby Road](/wiki/Anlaby_Road "Anlaby Road") would be built to become the club's first permanent home, opening in March 1906.[[271]](#cite_note-Anlaby_Road-271) The Tigers played there until they were forced to suspend their operations in 1941 due to the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[271]](#cite_note-Anlaby_Road-271)

When league football resumed in 1946, Hull moved into the newly built [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park").[[14]](#cite_note-Boothferry_Park-14) First planned in 1929, the stadium had seen slow development due to financial difficulty and the impact of the war on the city, particularly the [Hull Blitz](/wiki/Hull_Blitz "Hull Blitz").[[272]](#cite_note-A_History_of_Boothferry_Park-272) In 1951, [Boothferry Halt](/wiki/Boothferry_Park_Halt_railway_station "Boothferry Park Halt railway station") railway station opened to serve supporters travelling to the stadium. It would ultimately close in 1986 because of safety concerns.[[273]](#cite_note-BP_Halt-273)

Boothferry Park would be the Tigers' home for 56 years before they left for the new [KC Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium") in December 2002.[[14]](#cite_note-Boothferry_Park-14) By this time, the old ground had fallen into disrepair and the [Taylor Report](/wiki/Taylor_Report "Taylor Report") had enforced its ruling that clubs in the top two divisions of English football must have [all-seater stadiums](/wiki/All-seater_stadium "All-seater stadium").[[274]](#cite_note-274) Although Hull fell outside the restrictions at the time, playing in the fourth tier, it showed the club's ambition to progress.

City were joined at the new ground by [Hull F.C.](/wiki/Hull_F.C. "Hull F.C.") of the [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League"). In 2006, the stadium was named "Best Ground" at the inaugural [EFL Awards](/wiki/EFL_Awards "EFL Awards").[[275]](#cite_note-275) When its sponsor, the [KCOM Group](/wiki/KCOM_Group "KCOM Group"), rebranded in 2016, the stadium was renamed to the KCOM Stadium.[[276]](#cite_note-276) It was again renamed in 2021, becoming the MKM Stadium when MKM Building Supplies bought the stadium's naming rights.[[277]](#cite_note-277)

### Rivalries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Hull_City_supporters.jpg/250px-Hull_City_supporters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hull_City_supporters.jpg)

Hull City supporters at the celebrations on the team's promotion to the Premier League in 2008

*Sources*:[[278]](#cite_note-278)[[279]](#cite_note-279)[[280]](#cite_note-280)

Hull City are one of very few clubs in [English football](/wiki/Football_in_England "Football in England") to have no clear rival. They do contest the [Humber derby](/wiki/Humber_derby "Humber derby") with both [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") and [Scunthorpe United](/wiki/Scunthorpe_United_F.C. "Scunthorpe United F.C."), however Hull supporters tend to see [Leeds United](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C. "Leeds United F.C.") as bigger opponents than either of the two [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire") teams. On the other hand, Leeds fans consider [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") as their main rivals, with whom they contest the [Roses rivalry](/wiki/Leeds_United_F.C.%E2%80%93Manchester_United_F.C._rivalry "Leeds United F.C.–Manchester United F.C. rivalry"). Additionally, both [Lincoln City](/wiki/Lincoln_City_F.C. "Lincoln City F.C.") and [York City](/wiki/York_City_F.C. "York City F.C.") fans hold unrequited beliefs that Hull are their main rivals.

Some older Hull supporters also view [Sheffield United](/wiki/Sheffield_United_F.C. "Sheffield United F.C.") as minor rivals. This dates back to the [1983–84](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_in_English_football#Third_Division "1983–84 in English football") season, when the Blades won promotion at Hull's expense. The teams had ended up level on points and goal difference; however, the Tigers had scored an inferior number of goals. Furthermore, 33 of the [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire") side's goals had been scored by former Hull striker [Keith Edwards](/wiki/Keith_Edwards_(footballer,_born_1957) "Keith Edwards (footballer, born 1957)"). To make matters worse, Hull had even had fate in their own hands ahead of their final game of the season away at [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C."). The match had been rescheduled due to bad weather, and so took place after the rest of the league had finished their campaigns. Knowing they had to win by more than two goals, they could only manage a 2–0 victory in front of a crowd which included a number of Sheffield United fans, and lost out on promotion in agonising fashion.

### Fanbase

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/FA_Cup_Final_2014_Wembley_stadium.jpg/250px-FA_Cup_Final_2014_Wembley_stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:FA_Cup_Final_2014_Wembley_stadium.jpg)

Hull City supporters prior to the [2014 FA Cup final](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final") against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.")

Hull City have a handful of notable celebrity fans. Among them are decorated actors [Tom Courtenay](/wiki/Tom_Courtenay "Tom Courtenay") and [Omar Sharif](/wiki/Omar_Sharif "Omar Sharif").[[281]](#cite_note-281) Courtenay was born and raised in the local area,[[282]](#cite_note-282) and introduced Sharif to the Tigers when the two became friends in the 1960s. Sharif would reportedly follow the team via the television at his home in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"). If at any time he was not there and a match was being played, he would instead telephone the club's automated phoneline for score updates. The Egyptian was later awarded an honorary degree from the [University of Hull](/wiki/University_of_Hull "University of Hull") in 2010. Alongside Courtenay, he also became good friends with ex-player [Ken Wagstaff](/wiki/Ken_Wagstaff "Ken Wagstaff").[[283]](#cite_note-283) Coincidentally, Courtenay had previously owned a [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatian_dog "Dalmatian dog") named “Waggy” as a tribute to the forward.[[284]](#cite_note-284)

[John Prescott](/wiki/John_Prescott "John Prescott"), who was the [Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Deputy Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") from 1997 to 2007, became a supporter after spending most of his life in Hull.[[285]](#cite_note-285) After graduating from the University of Hull in his youth, he would go on to represent the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") in [Hull East](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull_East_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Kingston upon Hull East (UK Parliament constituency)") for nearly 40 years. Following his death in 2024, Prescott's funeral service was conducted at [Hull Minster](/wiki/Hull_Minster "Hull Minster").[[286]](#cite_note-286) [Mark Herman](/wiki/Mark_Herman "Mark Herman"), best known for writing and directing the 2008 film *[The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas](/wiki/The_Boy_in_the_Striped_Pyjamas_(film) "The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas (film)")*, is another Hull supporter, having been born in nearby [Bridlington](/wiki/Bridlington "Bridlington"). His schoolfriend, actor [Henry Priestman](/wiki/Henry_Priestman "Henry Priestman"), is also a fan of the club. The pair have even worked together to create [two songs](#Songs) about the Tigers, as well as a short documentary film.[[287]](#cite_note-287) [Roy North](/wiki/Roy_North "Roy North"), an actor and television presenter who is best known for being the companion of [Basil Brush](/wiki/Basil_Brush "Basil Brush") between 1973 and 1976, sometimes wore the Tigers' home shirts during broadcasts to show his love for his hometown club.[[288]](#cite_note-288) Elsewhere in entertainment, playwright and screenwriter [Alan Plater](/wiki/Alan_Plater "Alan Plater") grew up locally, having been born in [Jarrow](/wiki/Jarrow "Jarrow"), and became a Hull supporter as a result.[[289]](#cite_note-289) The youngest person to [run the length of Great Britain](/wiki/Land%27s_End_to_John_o%27_Groats "Land's End to John o' Groats") is also a Hull fan. In May 2025, [Marcus Skeet](/wiki/Marcus_Skeet "Marcus Skeet"), who reflects his loyalties in his online alias as *The Hull Boy*, was just over 17 years old when he completed the journey in only 58 days.[[290]](#cite_note-290) British singer [Sinitta](/wiki/Sinitta "Sinitta") began supporting Hull after being convinced to follow them through an ex-boyfriend who came from the area.[[291]](#cite_note-291) Another Tigers' fan from the music industry is [Stephen Malkmus](/wiki/Stephen_Malkmus "Stephen Malkmus"), lead singer of the band [Pavement](/wiki/Pavement_(band) "Pavement (band)"). Malkmus, although born and raised in [California](/wiki/California "California"), adopted Hull as his team after some of his band's technical crew introduced him to their local side.[[292]](#cite_note-292)

Aside from celebrity fans, the club is believed to have had a [hooligan firm](/wiki/Football_hooliganism "Football hooliganism") which dates back to the 1960s. As noted by Andy Nicholls and Nick Lowles, in their book *Hooligans: The A–L of Britain's Football Hooligan Gangs*, they were known as the [Hull City Psychos](/wiki/Hull_City_Psychos "Hull City Psychos").[[293]](#cite_note-293)

### Songs

Like several other clubs around England, Hull City have adopted the 1961 [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") song "[Can't Help Falling in Love](/wiki/Can%27t_Help_Falling_in_Love "Can't Help Falling in Love")" as a pre-match anthem.[[294]](#cite_note-294) Within the stands, a popular chant of "You're getting mauled by the Tigers!" is sung by Hull supporters when the team are winning. However, this chant often faces ridicule from opposing fanbases due to its "cringeworthy" nature.[[295]](#cite_note-295)

In February 1983, amidst the club's [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_in_English_football "1982–83 in English football") promotion campaign, celebrity fans [Henry Priestman](/wiki/Henry_Priestman "Henry Priestman") and [Mark Herman](/wiki/Mark_Herman "Mark Herman") released a song titled "The Tigers are Back". Working under the [pseudonyms](/wiki/Pseudonym "Pseudonym") of 'Harry Amber' and 'Mark Black' (together as 'Amber and Black'), the track featured backing vocals provided by various members of the Hull squad. This was done to help raise funds in order to pay the players' wages, as the club were suffering financially at the time. Herman reworded the song "Out of Luck" by Priestman's previous band [Yachts](/wiki/Yachts_(band) "Yachts (band)"), to get the lyrics. Both the record sleeves and the records themselves contained the logo of the fictional record label 'Don Records' alongside the fictional issue number 'COL001'. These were in tribute to [Don Robinson](/wiki/Don_Robinson_(British_businessman) "Don Robinson (British businessman)") and [Colin Appleton](/wiki/Colin_Appleton "Colin Appleton"), who, at the time, were the club's chairman and manager respectively.[[26]](#cite_note-A&B-26)

Exactly 25 years later, during another promotion campaign, this time the [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "2007–08 Hull City A.F.C. season") season, 'Amber and Black' (now stylised as 'Amber & Black') returned to record a new song. "The City's on Fire" released on [MySpace](/wiki/Myspace "Myspace") on 1 January 2008.[[296]](#cite_note-296) It was later re-released just before the [2014 FA Cup final](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final").[[26]](#cite_note-A&B-26)[[297]](#cite_note-297)[[298]](#cite_note-298)

### Name change

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Assem_Allam_1.png/250px-Assem_Allam_1.png)](/wiki/File:Assem_Allam_1.png)

[Assem Allam](/wiki/Assem_Allam "Assem Allam"), owner of Hull City between 2010 and 2022

#### 2013: Initial application

In August 2013, owner Assem Allam announced that the club had re-registered as "Hull City Tigers Ltd," and that the team would be marketed as "Hull City Tigers," removing the "Association Football Club" that had been part of the name since the club's formation in 1904.[[299]](#cite_note-299)[[300]](#cite_note-300) Vice-chairman [Ehab Allam](/wiki/Ehab_Allam "Ehab Allam") said "AFC" would remain on the club badge for the [2013–14 season](/wiki/2013-14_Premier_League "2013-14 Premier League"), but be removed after.[[301]](#cite_note-protest-301)

In response, a Premier League spokesman said, "We have not been informed of a change in the name of the actual club. They will still be known as Hull City as far as the Premier League is concerned when results or fixtures are published."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to its chairman, by 2014, the club would be further renamed "Hull Tigers," because, as he claimed, "in marketing, the shorter the name the more powerful [it is],"[[302]](#cite_note-id-302) while "Association Football Club" made the name too long. Allam stated he dislikes the word "City", as it is too "common" and a "lousy identity", since it is associated also with other clubs, such as Leicester City, Bristol City and Manchester City.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] He told [David Conn](/wiki/David_Conn "David Conn") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* that "in a few years many clubs will follow and change their names to something more interesting and I will have proved I am a leader,"[[302]](#cite_note-id-302) adding that if he were the owner of Manchester City, he would change their name to "Manchester Hunter."[[302]](#cite_note-id-302)

Allam justified the intended name change as part of his plans to create "additional sources of revenue" for the club, after [Hull City Council](/wiki/Hull_City_Council "Hull City Council") refused to sell him the stadium [freehold](/wiki/Freehold_(law) "Freehold (law)") so he could develop, as he had stated, "a sports park" on the site.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The council has refused to sell in order, as they stated, "to preserve the annual [Hull Fair](/wiki/Hull_Fair "Hull Fair") held on the adjacent car park."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After the collapse of the negotiations, Allam stated: "I had in mind £30 million to spend on the infrastructure of the club, to increase the stadium by 10,000 and to have commercial activities around the stadium — cafeterias, shops, supermarkets — to have all this to create income for the club so that in the future it can be self-financing and not relying on me." He asked [rhetorically](/wiki/Rhetorical_question "Rhetorical question"), "What if I dropped dead tomorrow?"[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Supporters' groups expressed opposition to the name change. Bernard Noble, chairman of Hull City's official supporters club said he was disappointed, although he agreed that Allam had saved the club from liquidation and that it was "his club".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Blogger Rick Skelton called the name change "a pointless exercise" and said, "Mr Allam's assertion that the name 'Hull City' is irrelevant and too common, is as disgusting a use of the English language as his new name for the club."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Before the first home match of the season on 24 August 2013, a group of supporters marched in protest against the name change.[[301]](#cite_note-protest-301) Allam dismissed complaints by fans.[[303]](#cite_note-dismiss-303)

In a comment published on 1 December 2013 in *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* in response to supporters' chants and banners of "City Till We Die", Allam said, "They can die as soon as they want, as long as they leave the club for the majority who just want to watch good football."[[304]](#cite_note-304) The supporters responded with chants of "We're Hull City, we'll die when we want" during that day's home match against [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."). Manager Steve Bruce credited the controversy for creating " a fantastic atmosphere" but added, "I have got to have a conversation with him because I don't think he quite understands what it means in terms of history and tradition."[[305]](#cite_note-305) However, Bruce also said that, because of the money Allam had invested in the club, "If he thinks Hull Tigers is his way forward then we have to respect it."[[306]](#cite_note-306)

On 11 December 2013, a spokesman for Hull City announced that the club had formally applied to the [Football Association](/wiki/Football_Association "Football Association") to have its name changed to "Hull Tigers" from the 2014–15 season onwards.[[307]](#cite_note-307) The [FA Council](/wiki/FA_Council "FA Council"), which has "absolute discretion" in deciding whether to approve the plan or not, stated the next day that it would follow a "consultation process" with [stakeholders](/wiki/Stakeholder_(corporate)#Applications_of_the_term "Stakeholder (corporate)"), "including the club's supporter groups".[[308]](#cite_note-Conn_12122013-308)

#### 2014: Resistance and rejection

Some brand and marketing experts came out in support of the name change.[[309]](#cite_note-supp-309) Simon Chadwick, professor of Sport Business Strategy and Marketing at the [Coventry University Business School](/wiki/Coventry_University_Business_School "Coventry University Business School"), cautiously opined that the objective of opening up lucrative new markets for shirt sales, merchandise and broadcast deals shows commercial vision and could bring benefits.[[309]](#cite_note-supp-309) [David Stern](/wiki/David_Stern "David Stern"), [commissioner](/wiki/Commissioner_of_the_NBA "Commissioner of the NBA") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") in the United States, warned: "I would say a wise owner [of a sports club] would view his ownership as something of a public trust, in addition to the profit motive, and you really do want to allow the fans a little bit more input than I think is being allowed, with respect to Hull."[[309]](#cite_note-supp-309)

On 17 March 2014, the FA membership committee advised that the re application be rejected at the FA Council meeting on 9 April.[[310]](#cite_note-310) In response, the club published a statement saying the FA was "prejudiced", and criticised the committee's consultation with the City Till We Die opposition group.[[311]](#cite_note-311) The following week, the club opened a ballot of [season ticket holders](/wiki/Season_ticket#Sports "Season ticket") over the name change. Opponents of the name change criticised as "loaded" the questions, which asked respondents to choose between "Yes to Hull Tigers with the Allam family continuing to lead the club", "No to Hull Tigers" and "I am not too concerned and will continue to support the club either way", on the grounds that voters were not given the option to reject the name while keeping the Allam family as owners.[[312]](#cite_note-312) Of 15,033 season ticket holders, 5,874 voted in all, with 2,565 voting in favour of the change and 2,517 against, while 792 chose the "not too concerned" option.[[313]](#cite_note-313)

On 9 April 2014, the FA Council announced its decision, carried by a 63.5% vote of its members, to reject the club's application for a name change.[[314]](#cite_note-rejects-314) The club's owner, Assam Allem, responded by stating it would appeal the decision.[[314]](#cite_note-rejects-314) However, since there was no appeal process with the FA and its council, the decision was final. On 11 September 2014, Allam mentioned that an appeal against the FA's ruling was being sent to the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport"). He also held a news conference confirming the club had been put up for sale due to the English FA's decision on 9 April 2014.[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[315]](#cite_note-315)

In October 2014, interviewed by the BBC, Allam confirmed that he would "not invest a penny more in the club" unless he is allowed to change the club's name to Hull Tigers.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[316]](#cite_note-pennymore-316) In the same interview, Allam said, "I have never been a football fan. I am still not a football fan. I am a community fan."[[316]](#cite_note-pennymore-316)

#### 2015: Re-application

In March 2015, an independent panel appointed by the Court of Arbitration for Sport ruled that the decision of the Football Association Council to block the name change was invalidated by flaws in the process.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[317]](#cite_note-reapp-317)

In July 2015, the Football Supporters Federation confirmed that a 70/30 decision was made in favour of Hull City A.F.C. not changing their name after an FA vote.[[318]](#cite_note-318)

## Finances

In the club's [annual report](/wiki/Annual_report "Annual report") for the 12-month period up to 31 July 2009, [auditors](/wiki/Auditor "Auditor") [Deloitte](/wiki/Deloitte "Deloitte") stated that [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")4.4 million had gone out of the club and stadium company to owner [Russell Bartlett](/wiki/Russell_Bartlett "Russell Bartlett")'s holding companies in loans, while at least £2.9 million of it was used in the take-over itself of the club.[[319]](#cite_note-blo-319) A further £560,000 was paid, according to the audit, by the stadium company to Bartlett's holding companies in "management fees," while at least £1 million was owed to him personally as a "salary".[[319]](#cite_note-blo-319) After the warning from Deloitte, Bartlett gave the club a £4 million loan,[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*][[320]](#cite_note-320) "which brought the money he had taken out and put in since taking over to about even."[[319]](#cite_note-blo-319)

The [corporate entity](/wiki/Corporation "Corporation") that owns the football club, "The Hull City Association Football Club (Tigers) Ltd," is currently owned by Allamhouse Limited, a private, [limited-liability](/wiki/Limited_liability "Limited liability") company with a [share capital](/wiki/Share_capital "Share capital") of £10 million (as of October 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hull_City_A.F.C.&action=edit)),[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[321]](#cite_note-who-321)[[322]](#cite_note-322) registered in [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey").[[319]](#cite_note-blo-319) The [beneficial owners](/wiki/Beneficial_owner "Beneficial owner") of Allamhouse Limited, established in 2009,[[323]](#cite_note-cos-323) are the Allam family.[[321]](#cite_note-who-321)

On an "Opacity Score" out of 100, where zero indicates complete openness and 100 complete secrecy, the company which owns the club has been rated by [Christian Aid](/wiki/Christian_Aid "Christian Aid") at 87.[[319]](#cite_note-blo-319)

Hull City's [corporate accounts](/wiki/Bookkeeping "Bookkeeping"), as of July 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hull_City_A.F.C.&action=edit), showed a £25.6 million loss, on [revenues](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue") of £11 million, after player and management costs of "just under £23 million."[[324]](#cite_note-eye-324) The club has "[future tax losses](/wiki/Deferred_tax "Deferred tax")" available of more than £45 million.[[324]](#cite_note-eye-324) Another Assam Allam company, Allam Marine, also wholly owned by Allamhouse Limited, revealed in its 2012 accounts that "[utilisation of tax losses](/wiki/Tax_shelter "Tax shelter") from [group](/wiki/Corporate_group "Corporate group") companies" reduced its [tax liability](/wiki/United_Kingdom_corporation_tax "United Kingdom corporation tax") by £3.8 million over 2011 and 2012.[[324]](#cite_note-eye-324)

As reported,[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] [HM Revenue and Customs](/wiki/HM_Revenue_and_Customs "HM Revenue and Customs") are in the process of[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] an inquiry at Hull City AFC, as part of the British tax authorities' targeting of football clubs over "tax-free payments to players under [image rights](/wiki/Image_rights "Image rights")' deals and the provision of [benefits in kind](/wiki/Benefits_in_kind "Benefits in kind").[[324]](#cite_note-eye-324) For Hull City AFC, the provision for benefits in kind was reported at £682,000 as of July 2011, growing to £810,000 by July 2012.[[324]](#cite_note-eye-324)

## Players

As of the [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Professional_U21_Development_League "2024–25 Professional U21 Development League") season, Hull City's development squad compete in the [Professional Development League North Division](/wiki/Professional_Development_League "Professional Development League").[[340]](#cite_note-U21_fixtures-340) The team's home matches are played at various grounds, including the [MKM Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium"), but are usually played at the [LNER Community Stadium](/wiki/York_Community_Stadium "York Community Stadium") in [York](/wiki/York "York").[[340]](#cite_note-U21_fixtures-340)

### Hull City Ladies F.C.

Main article: [Hull City Ladies F.C.](/wiki/Hull_City_Ladies_F.C. "Hull City Ladies F.C.")

Hull City Ladies F.C. are not a registered affiliate of Hull City A.F.C. and their men's team, however, they do share a number of similarities, namely their club colours and crest. As of the [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_FA_Women%27s_National_League#Northern_Division "2024–25 FA Women's National League") season, they compete in the [FA Women's National League North](/wiki/FA_Women%27s_National_League_North "FA Women's National League North"). *The Tigresses*, as they are known, play their home games at the Easy Buy Stadium in nearby [Barton-upon-Humber](/wiki/Barton-upon-Humber "Barton-upon-Humber"), [Lincolnshire](/wiki/Lincolnshire "Lincolnshire").[[397]](#cite_note-407)

Further information: [List of Hull City A.F.C. records and statistics](/wiki/List_of_Hull_City_A.F.C._records_and_statistics "List of Hull City A.F.C. records and statistics")

:   *As of 8 August 2024*[[410]](#cite_note-Club_Records-426)[[411]](#cite_note-427)[[227]](#cite_note-Wigan_defeat-227)[[412]](#cite_note-428)

### Appearances

[Andy Davidson](/wiki/Andy_Davidson_(footballer) "Andy Davidson (footballer)") holds the record for the most appearances for Hull City. He featured 520 times for the Tigers in the league, and 579 times in all competitions. [Garreth Roberts](/wiki/Garreth_Roberts "Garreth Roberts") is second on the all competitions appearances list with 487, just ahead of [Chris Chilton](/wiki/Chris_Chilton "Chris Chilton") on 477. In the league alone, Roberts stands at 414, one behind Chilton on 415, who comes third. Between him and Davidson is [George Maddison](/wiki/George_Maddison_(footballer,_born_1902) "George Maddison (footballer, born 1902)"), with 430 league appearances.

The youngest debutant the club has seen was [Matt Edeson](/wiki/Matt_Edeson "Matt Edeson"). At 16 years and 60 days old, he came off the bench against [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham_F.C. "Fulham F.C.") on 10 October 1992.[[q]](#cite_note-430) Hull's oldest debutant is [Andy Hessenthaler](/wiki/Andy_Hessenthaler "Andy Hessenthaler"), who played against [Chesterfield](/wiki/Chesterfield_F.C. "Chesterfield F.C.") on 1 February 2005 at 39 years and 168 days old. However, [Steve Harper](/wiki/Steve_Harper "Steve Harper") was the oldest player to appear for the Tigers. On 24 May 2015, at 40 years and 60 days old, Harper was in goal for the 0–0 draw against [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") that saw Hull relegated from the [Premier League](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Premier_League_season "2014–15 Premier League season").

[Theodore Whitmore](/wiki/Theodore_Whitmore "Theodore Whitmore") of [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica_national_football_team "Jamaica national football team") holds the record for the most international caps won whilst playing for Hull City. During his time in black and amber between 1999 and 2002, Whitmore amassed 28 caps for his nation.

### Goals

[Chris Chilton](/wiki/Chris_Chilton "Chris Chilton") holds the record for the most goals for Hull City. He scored 222 goals in all competitions between 1960 and 1971, with 193 of those being league goals. [Ken Wagstaff](/wiki/Ken_Wagstaff "Ken Wagstaff"), Chilton's strike partner for much of that time, comes second on both lists, with 173 of his 197 goals in black and amber coming in the league. [Sammy Stevens](/wiki/Sammy_Stevens "Sammy Stevens") is third in the all competitions list with 116 goals, whereas [Paddy Mills](/wiki/Paddy_Mills "Paddy Mills") and his 101 goals ranks him third amongst the league goalscorers.

The club record for the most goals scored by a single player in a competitive fixture belongs to [Duane Darby](/wiki/Duane_Darby "Duane Darby"). Against [Whitby Town](/wiki/Whitby_Town_F.C. "Whitby Town F.C.") in an [FA Cup](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_FA_Cup#First_round_proper "1996–97 FA Cup") first round replay on 26 November 1996, Darby scored six times in an 8–4 goalfest at [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park"). The club record for the most goals scored by a single player in a single season is the 42 goals [Bill McNaughton](/wiki/Bill_McNaughton "Bill McNaughton") scored in the [1932–33](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Hull_City_A.F.C._season "1932–33 Hull City A.F.C. season") season.

### Scorelines

Hull City's biggest margin of victory in a competitive fixture was their 11–1 drubbing of [Carlisle United](/wiki/Carlisle_United_F.C. "Carlisle United F.C.") at [Anlaby Road](/wiki/Anlaby_Road "Anlaby Road") on 14 January 1939. The Tigers' biggest competitive away win was their 8–2 thrashing of [Stalybridge Celtic](/wiki/Stalybridge_Celtic_F.C. "Stalybridge Celtic F.C.") on 26 November 1932 in the first round of the [FA Cup](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_FA_Cup#First_round_proper "1932–33 FA Cup").

Hull have been beaten 8–0 twice in their history, which is their biggest ever losing margin in a competitive fixture. Firstly, they lost to [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") on 4 November 1911. This humiliating defeat was then equalled over a century later when [Wigan Athletic](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C.") beat the Tigers on [14 July 2020](/wiki/Wigan_Athletic_F.C._8%E2%80%930_Hull_City_A.F.C. "Wigan Athletic F.C. 8–0 Hull City A.F.C.").

### Attendances

The record for the highest ever attended competitive fixture involving Hull City stands at 89,345 spectators. This was set on 17 May 2014 for the [2014 FA Cup final](/wiki/2014_FA_Cup_final "2014 FA Cup final") against [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), which ended with the Tigers being beaten 3–2 in extra-time.

Hull's highest ever home attendance for a competitive fixture was recorded on 26 February 1949, when a crowd of 55,019 attended an [FA Cup](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_FA_Cup "1948–49 FA Cup") sixth round tie against [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") at [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park"). Since moving to the [MKM Stadium](/wiki/MKM_Stadium "MKM Stadium"), the record stands at 25,030 spectators. This was set on 9 May 2010, when the Tigers played out a 0–0 draw with [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") on the final day of the [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Premier_League_season "2009–10 Premier League season") season.

### Transfers

The largest transfer fee the club has ever paid for a player is the £13 million they paid [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur_F.C. "Tottenham Hotspur F.C.") for [Ryan Mason](/wiki/Ryan_Mason "Ryan Mason") in 2016. Conversely, the most they have ever received for a player is the £22 million [West Ham United](/wiki/West_Ham_United_F.C. "West Ham United F.C.") bought [Jarrod Bowen](/wiki/Jarrod_Bowen "Jarrod Bowen") for in 2020.
